sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 30-year-old female, presented with a chronic history of asthma symptoms, specifically coughing and wheezing, which have been persistent over the past six months.
She reports that her symptoms worsen with respiratory infections and that she has had to frequently visit urgent care and her primary care provider for exacerbations.
She has been using an albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) for relief, taking two puffs as needed, but has had difficulty managing her symptoms.
She has had one hospitalization for asthma exacerbation as a child, but none since then.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of asthma, for which she has been using an albuterol inhaler as needed.
Review of systems:
The patient reports no other symptoms, but notes that she has trouble with asthma exacerbations when she is around her aunt's cat.
Current medications:
Albuterol (Ventolin) 2 puffs as needed; Flovent 110 micrograms 1 puff twice per day (newly prescribed).
Objective:
Vital signs: blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam:
The patient's lung exam revealed diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
The neck exam was unremarkable, with a midline trachea and no lymphadenopathy.
The patient's hands had strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing, and brisk capillary refill.
Test Results:
Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has moderate intermittent asthma; Status: active.
Problem list: Asthma, status: active.
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms 1 puff twice per day to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for rescue inhaler use.
The patient can continue using her albuterol inhaler (Ventolin) 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage symptoms and exacerbations.
Follow-up:
I would like to see the patient in a month or sooner if she experiences worsening symptoms.
